skill.name: azure-ai-projects-py
skill.description: Build Python applications with Azure AI Projects, Microsoft Foundry project clients, versioned agents, evaluations, and OpenAI-compatible calls.
skill.summary: Build Azure AI Projects apps with Foundry clients, agents, evaluations, and project resources.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution hits, but review found they are Markdown backticks, code fences, and SDK examples rather than executable skill logic. The Azure endpoint and environment-variable examples are legitimate configuration patterns, and no prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found. Risk remains low because this community skill guides users through cloud SDK operations that can access Azure project resources.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported Ruby or shell backtick hits are Markdown inline code, fenced code blocks, and SDK member names. No executable Ruby code, shell command interpolation, or skill-side command runner was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review confirms the flagged syntax is Markdown documentation and Python examples. There is no Ruby backtick execution or shell interpolation in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Azure Endpoint and Environment Variable Examples
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE but LOW_RISK: The skill shows users how to read Azure project endpoints and model deployment names from environment variables. This is normal SDK configuration, but users should not paste real secrets or sensitive endpoints into prompts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents AZURE_AI_PROJECT_ENDPOINT and os.environ usage. The context is legitimate Azure SDK setup rather than credential harvesting or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography hit points to front matter describing Azure AI Projects. No weak cryptographic algorithm, hashing function, cipher selection, or cryptographic API use was found at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is descriptive metadata about the SDK. It contains no cryptographic operation or security-sensitive algorithm choice.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported dangerous combination depends on other false positives and benign configuration examples. Review found no obfuscation, hidden instructions, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection attempt in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is a readable Markdown SDK guide. The network and environment references support normal Azure client setup and are not combined with executable exfiltration logic.

content.value_statement: Azure AI Projects applications require many SDK objects, clients, and resource patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for building Python apps on Microsoft Foundry.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install azure-ai-projects with azure-identity.
Demonstrates native Foundry clients and OpenAI-compatible client usage.
Provides examples for creating agents, versioned agents, threads, messages, and runs.
Covers listing connections, deployments, datasets, indexes, and memory stores.
Introduces evaluation runs with built-in criteria through the OpenAI-compatible client.
limitations: The scanned package contains only SKILL.md, although it references additional files.
It does not include runnable sample projects or automated tests.
It assumes the user already has an Azure AI Foundry project and permissions.
It gives high-level examples and does not replace official SDK API documentation.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Foundry Agent
use_cases.description: Create a Python agent using AIProjectClient, a model deployment, instructions, and a simple run flow.

use_cases.title: Inventory Project Resources
use_cases.description: List project connections, deployments, datasets, and indexes before wiring them into an application.

use_cases.title: Add Evaluation Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the OpenAI-compatible client to start evaluation runs and apply built-in quality criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show me how to create an AIProjectClient with DefaultAzureCredential and an Azure AI project endpoint.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Agent Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a Python flow that creates an Azure AI Projects agent, starts a thread, sends a message, and reads the response.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Client Approaches
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to compare AIProjectClient and the OpenAI-compatible client for my Azure AI Projects application.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a production-ready Azure AI Projects pattern with versioned agents, connections, evaluations, and cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: Create a basic Azure AI Projects client and agent.
output: A short setup checklist for package installation and environment variables.
A Python-oriented implementation plan for the client and agent lifecycle.
Notes about credential setup, model deployment names, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: Help me understand which project resources my app can use.
output: A categorized explanation of connections, deployments, datasets, indexes, and memory stores.
Guidance on when each resource type belongs in an agent workflow.
Warnings about validating Azure permissions before running project operations.

output_examples.input: Plan an evaluation workflow for a Foundry agent.
output: A step-by-step evaluation plan using built-in criteria.
Recommended checkpoints for test data, scoring, and review.
Follow-up tasks for improving prompts and agent versions.

best_practices: Store Azure endpoints and deployment names in environment variables or secure configuration.
Use versioned agents when deploying behavior that must be reviewed or rolled back.
Clean up test agents, threads, and project resources after experiments.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode production endpoints, tenant details, or credentials in shared prompts.
Do not assume every referenced tool is enabled in the current Azure project.
Do not use quick prototype flows as production code without error handling and access review.

faq.question: What SDK does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers the Azure AI Projects Python SDK, also named azure-ai-projects.

faq.question: Is this the same as azure-ai-agents?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses on the higher-level Foundry project SDK, not the lower-level agents SDK.

faq.question: Does it support OpenAI-compatible calls?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to get an OpenAI-compatible client from an Azure AI project client.

faq.question: Can it help with versioned agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes PromptAgentDefinition guidance for creating versioned agents.

faq.question: Does it include complete runnable examples?
faq.answer: No. The scanned package contains guidance in SKILL.md, not a full sample application.

faq.question: What should users prepare first?
faq.answer: Users need an Azure AI Foundry project, a model deployment, and suitable Azure credentials.
